title: Spectral Statistics of Lévy Matrices
speaker: Patrick Lopatto
abstract: Lévy matrices are symmetric random matrices whose entries are independent alpha-stable laws. Such distributions have infinite variance, and when alpha is less than 1, infinite mean. In the latter case these matrices are conjectured to exhibit a sharp transition from a delocalized regime at low energy to a localized regime at high energy, like the infamous Anderson model in mathematical physics. We discuss work establishing the existence of a delocalized regime with GOE eigenvalue statistics.

proof_stages.label: Baseline question and Lévy matrix model
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from eigenvector distributions for GOE and finite-variance Wigner matrices, then defines α-stable heavy-tailed symmetric Lévy matrices with n^{-1/α} scaling and the intuition of rare large entries plus small noise.

proof_stages.label: Non-Gaussian small-energy eigenvector statistics
proof_stages.summary: For the median and, more generally, small eigenvalues, individual eigenvector coordinates converge not to a fixed Gaussian law but to Gaussian mixtures with random scale, and nearby eigenvectors have correlations absent in GOE.

proof_stages.label: Spectral notions and conjectural phase diagram
proof_stages.summary: After introducing the heavy-tailed limiting spectral density, delocalization, and local eigenvalue statistics, the speaker states the physics prediction of GOE/delocalized behavior below a mobility edge and Poisson/localized behavior above it when α<1.

proof_stages.label: Rigorous delocalized-GOE regimes
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem proves the predicted delocalized GOE phase for 1<α<2 and proves, for all but a countable exceptional set of α, a nonexplicit small-energy regime with complete delocalization and GOE local statistics.

proof_stages.label: Position among localization-transition models
proof_stages.summary: The results are positioned against earlier partial results, Anderson-type transition questions, and finite-variance random-matrix universality classes, emphasizing that Lévy matrices display genuinely new heavy-tailed behavior.

proof_stages.label: Finite-variance three-step universality machinery
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the Stieltjes transform and local laws, explains how diagonal resolvent bounds yield delocalization, and recalls the universality strategy via Gaussian perturbation, Dyson Brownian motion short-time equilibrium, and comparison.

proof_stages.label: Lévy obstructions and small-large comparison
proof_stages.summary: The finite-variance method fails because moment matching, optimal local laws, and deterministic resolvent limits are unavailable, so the proof splits H=A+B into small and large entries, treats A as a Gaussian-like perturbation of B, proves a local law for B, applies DBM to B+√tW, and compares back to H.

proof_stages.label: Random resolvent fixed point behind eigenvector mixtures
proof_stages.summary: For eigenvector entries, a modified three-step argument shows that the random Gaussian scaling comes from nonconcentration of diagonal Green-function entries, whose heavy-tailed limit is characterized by a Schur-complement recursive distributional equation that can be solved explicitly at z=0.

examples.name: Dyson Brownian motion as nonintersecting random walks
examples.note: The DBM SDE is made concrete by first ignoring the repulsion term and then viewing the eigenvalues as Brownian particles pushed apart like charged particles, clarifying the GOE gap mechanism.
